nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered bays and inner reefs over fine rubble and sand, at depths of approximately 3–30 m

diet.notes: Tends to graze on filamentous algae, copepods, amphipods, and small worms in the sandbed. Has been observed accepting vitamin-enriched mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and high-quality pellet foods in captivity. Some individuals may initially require live baby brine shrimp or garlic-enhanced foods to transition onto prepared diets. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised.

summary: A small, peaceful reef-safe goby from the Indo-West Pacific, known for its yellow-striped pattern and sand-sifting behavior. Generally suitable for nano or small reef systems with an established sandbed.
careNotes:
Hector's Goby (Koumansetta hectori, known in older literature as Amblygobius hectori) is a benthic hover goby that tends to spend most of its time close to the substrate, sifting sand and grazing among rockwork for copepods, small worms, and filamentous algae. A fine sand substrate is generally recommended so the fish can exhibit natural foraging behavior. It has been reported to help reduce nuisance hair algae and flatworm populations in established reef systems.

A tight-fitting or weighted aquarium lid is strongly advised — this species has been documented jumping when startled, and escape events are not uncommon during the acclimation period.

The species is generally considered reef-safe with no documented tendency to nip corals or bother invertebrates. It can be kept singly or as a mated pair; conspecific aggression tends to increase if multiple individuals are housed in small enclosures. Bonded pairs have been known to cohabit peacefully, however.

In terms of captive breeding, ORA documented the first reported successful spawning and rearing of this species in 2014. The larvae require an exceptionally long settlement period of approximately 60 days, making commercial aquaculture challenging.
breedingNotes: ORA reported the first successful captive spawning in 2014. Larvae require an unusually long pelagic larval phase of approximately 60 days before settlement, which has made commercial rearing challenging. No widely documented protocol for hobbyist breeding exists.

additional.name: AquaInfo
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8.5 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), minimum tank size (100 L), native range, and diet details.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy (Gobiidae, Gobiiformes), maximum size 8.5 cm SL, native range Indo-West Pacific (Seychelles to Fiji, north to Japan, south to New Caledonia), habitat depth 3–30 m.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked salinity (1.020–1.025), temperature (72–78°F), minimum tank size listed as 30 gal. Confirmed peaceful temperament, reef safe, omnivore diet.

additional.name: BiomesInaBox Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.024, dKH 8–12, temp 72–78°F). Confirmed escape risk — tight-fitting lid required. Care level: moderate.

fish.breedingNotes: ORA reported the first successful captive rearing in 2014; larvae require ~60 days pelagic phase before settlement. No established hobbyist protocol.
